phases.title: Get your money ready
phases.summary: Before you start viewing, get your deposit, budget and credit in order. Everything after this moves faster once it's done.

steps.title: Work out what you can really afford
steps.blurb: Your true budget is the price plus all the extras — not just the deposit.

tasks.label: Confirm the deposit you'll put down (aim for 5–10%+ of the price; more unlocks better rates)
Budget the one-off extras: solicitor/conveyancer, survey, mortgage fee, removals, buildings insurance
Check your Stamp Duty — as an England first-time buyer you pay nothing on the first £300k if the price is £500k or below
tasks.note: First-time buyer relief: gov.uk Stamp Duty Land Tax calculator

tasks.label: Leave a contingency for moving-in costs and early repairs

steps.title: Get your deposit and savings working
steps.blurb: A bigger deposit steps you down through the 90 / 85 / 80 / 75% rate bands.

tasks.label: Confirm where the deposit is held and that it's accessible when you need it
If you hold a Lifetime ISA, check the bonus and the withdrawal rules before relying on it
Make sure the deposit has been in your account long enough to satisfy a lender

steps.title: Check and tidy your credit
steps.blurb: Lenders look closely at your credit history — small fixes now help.

tasks.label: Check your credit report on the main agencies and correct any errors
Make sure you're on the electoral roll at your current address
Pay down small debts and avoid new credit applications before you apply
Keep every bill paid on time in the months before applying

phases.title: Get a Mortgage in Principle
phases.summary: An MIP (also called an Agreement in Principle) shows agents and sellers you're a serious, credible buyer.

tasks.label: Photo ID and proof of address
Last 3 months' payslips and your latest P60 (or 2–3 years' accounts if self-employed)
Last 3 months' bank statements
Proof of deposit, plus proof of any gifted funds

steps.title: Decide how you'll borrow
steps.blurb: Understand the trade-offs before you commit to a deal.

tasks.label: Decide whether to use a mortgage broker or go direct to lenders
Compare fixed vs tracker rates and the fee-vs-rate trade-off
Note the LTV band your deposit puts you in (rates step down at 90 / 85 / 80 / 75%)
Apply for the MIP/AIP (a soft check that doesn't harm your credit)
Save the certificate and note its expiry (usually ~30–90 days)

phases.title: Find the right home
phases.summary: The fun part — but stay disciplined about your must-haves and view with a critical eye.

tasks.label: Separate your must-haves from your nice-to-haves
Set your target areas and your price ceiling

steps.title: Set up alerts and register with agents

tasks.label: Turn on portal alerts so new listings reach you fast
Register with local agents and tell them your position (first-time buyer, MIP in hand)

steps.title: Book and attend viewings
steps.blurb: View more than once, and at different times of day.

tasks.label: Book viewings for your shortlist
Visit at different times to gauge noise, traffic and parking
Book a second viewing for any front-runner, and measure the rooms

steps.title: Inspect each property properly
steps.blurb: A calm, thorough look now saves money and regret later.

tasks.label: Outside: roof, walls, gutters and drainage — look for cracks and damp
Inside: damp, mould, cracks, and any lingering smells or stains
Plumbing: run the taps, check water pressure, look for leaks
Electrics: test sockets and check the fuse box / last inspection date
Heating: boiler age and last service; check the radiators
Check the EPC rating and the broadband / mobile signal
Note which fixtures and fittings are included
Check recent sold prices nearby for a realistic value
Check schools, transport links and flood risk
Walk the neighbourhood and check the commute

phases.title: Make an offer
phases.summary: A well-judged offer, backed by your strong position, wins the right home at the right price.

steps.title: Decide your offer
steps.blurb: Base it on evidence, not emotion — sold comparables, condition, and time on the market.

tasks.label: Check recent sold prices for similar nearby homes
Factor in condition and any work the survey might later flag
Set your opening offer and your walk-away ceiling (your strategy: start ~5–10% under asking)
Make the offer through the agent and state your position (first-time buyer, chain-free, MIP ready)
Be ready to negotiate; know your maximum before you start
Get the agreed price and what's included in writing
Ask the agent to mark it Sold STC and take it off the market

phases.title: Offer accepted: instruct and apply
phases.summary: The clock now starts. Moving quickly here is the single biggest thing you control.

steps.title: Instruct a solicitor or conveyancer
steps.blurb: They handle the legal transfer. Responsiveness matters more than the cheapest quote.

tasks.label: Get two or three fixed-fee quotes and check they include searches
Instruct your choice and read the letter of engagement carefully
Complete the anti-money-laundering ID checks
Pay the money on account for searches
Submit the full mortgage application (via your broker or lender)
Expect the lender to run a mortgage valuation of the property
Reply fast to any questions or document requests

steps.title: Book the right survey
steps.blurb: Match the survey level to the property's age and condition.

tasks.label: For most modern homes, a Level 2 (HomeBuyer) survey is enough
For older, larger or unusual properties, choose a Level 3 (Building) survey
Book it and read the report carefully when it arrives
Get buildings-insurance quotes now
Arrange cover to start from your exchange date (not completion)

phases.title: Conveyancing: searches and enquiries
phases.summary: The longest, quietest stretch. Keep nudging everyone and reply quickly to keep it moving.

steps.title: Searches
steps.blurb: Your solicitor orders local, water/drainage and environmental searches — usually 2–6 weeks.

tasks.label: Confirm the searches have been ordered
Review the results with your solicitor and ask about anything flagged

steps.title: Review the survey and renegotiate if needed

tasks.label: Read the survey findings carefully
If it reveals problems, get repair quotes and consider renegotiating the price

steps.title: Pre-contract enquiries
steps.blurb: Your solicitor raises questions on the title and the seller's property forms.

tasks.label: Review the seller's property-information and fixtures forms
Make sure your solicitor's enquiries are answered to your satisfaction
Confirm the EPC is valid

steps.title: Get your mortgage offer in writing

tasks.label: Receive and check the formal mortgage offer
Confirm it matches the property and the agreed price

steps.title: Agree fixtures and a completion date

tasks.label: Agree what's included (fixtures & fittings) in writing
Agree a completion date with the seller (and any chain)

phases.title: Exchange contracts
phases.summary: Once you exchange, the sale is legally binding — the point of no return, in a good way.

steps.title: Final checks before exchange
steps.blurb: Run the checklist — everything should be in place before you commit.

tasks.label: Searches back and reviewed
Survey done and any issues resolved
Mortgage offer in writing
Deposit funds ready to transfer
Buildings insurance ready to start on exchange
Completion date agreed
Sign the contract
Transfer your exchange deposit to your solicitor
Solicitors exchange contracts — you're now legally committed
Confirm your buildings insurance is live from today

phases.title: Completion: get the keys
phases.summary: Completion day — the money moves, and the home becomes yours.

tasks.label: Your solicitor sends the balance to the seller's solicitor
Once the funds clear, the sale completes
Collect the keys from the agent

steps.title: The legal tidy-up (handled for you)
steps.blurb: Your conveyancer does these — just check they're done.

tasks.label: Stamp Duty is paid to HMRC within the deadline
You're registered as the new owner with HM Land Registry
You receive your title deeds and completion statement — keep them safe

phases.title: Move in and settle
phases.summary: The keys are yours. Here's how to land smoothly in your new home.

tasks.label: Book removals 4–6 weeks ahead (or plan a DIY move)
Buy packing materials and start decluttering
Pack non-essentials early and label boxes by room
Make an inventory and photograph anything valuable
Book broadband (mind the lead time)
Tell people your new address: bank, DVLA, GP, employer, electoral roll
Take meter readings — gas, electric, water
Locate the stopcock and test it
Pack a first-night box: chargers, meds, documents, kettle, bedding
Set up gas, electricity and water with suppliers
Register for council tax
Change the locks
File your completion statement and title deeds somewhere safe
